title: Canon tiers DotA analysis and the meaning of robot
display_title: Canon tiers DotA analysis and the meaning of robot
wikitext:
[]: Canon tiers, DotA, analysis, and the meaning of robot             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Sep 11, 2014 12:50 am Isn't the actual material you people cite from that game about five paragraphs worth of stuff anyway? Things that matter a lot to fans are usually irrelevant to creators. Example: Nobody working on Star Trek cared how the Heisenberg Compensator works (very well, thank you) it was there for the sake of the story and the bullshit technobabble was made up after the fact. Roddenberry was making a show about social issues, he didn't give a shit about how obviously impossible and illogical technology would work. Anno made a show about the ordeal of depression and the pain of the struggle to move forward that doubles as a hypersigil to free himself from depression and move otaku culture forward that blew up in his face because of all the energy piled into his creation by all the wank sessions sparked by that shot of Misato with her boobies out in the intro.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Thu Sep 11, 2014 12:51 am Yes, and it's five paragraphs of stuff that's pretty darn important. It's also second-tier canon.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Sep 11, 2014 12:54 am Nuclear Lunchbox wrote: Yes, and it's five paragraphs of stuff that's pretty darn important.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Thu Sep 11, 2014 12:55 am Star Trek's not a good comparison, since it involved many hands in the pot, paid little heed to continuity, and blatantly contradicted itself on many occasions. NGE's far more consistent; it clearly changed direction midway through, but apart from a couple of notable bits (24') it doesn't contradict its own lore all that much. And yeah, it's like five paragraphs, but it's five paragraphs Anno wrote. He didn't just approve it, it's his material. Chuckman wrote: If it was important it would be addressed in the show.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Thu Sep 11, 2014 12:57 am Just because the show doesn't address something is no reason to write off its supplemental materials. For example, the glyphs in Madoka Magica are never explained, but they're pretty interesting (and pretty darn important in Rebellion) when translated.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Sep 11, 2014 1:00 am NGE has a distinct made-up-as-they-went-along feel to me. First time I watched the show I mainlined it and when they brought out stuff like the spear I was like "what the fuck is that and where did it come from" but when I realized how channeled and self-organizing the whole work is I understood. I'd bet you a billion earth dubloons that when Anno put Rei in the first episode he didn't know how or why she was there either. @Nuke: No, really. If the NGE2 stuff was that important they'd have worked it in. It's not that much information. If anything the vagueness within the show and film enhances the awe and spectacle of what's going on.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Thu Sep 11, 2014 1:01 am Chuckman wrote: NGE has a distinct made-up-as-they-went-along feel to me.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Sep 11, 2014 1:03 am Bagheera wrote: It's all referenced in the show.

[]:             Quote Post by ThanatosII » Thu Sep 11, 2014 1:14 am I think we can also consider how Evangelion's story kept leading you on and expanded its story doing so. For instance, we're at first never sure why Eva Unit 01 keeps going berserk, but then we eventually get an expansive story to explain the whole parent-to-child pilot link. We're first told Second Impact was because of a meteorite, then we find out it had to do with something with Angels, and then we found out SEELE had its hand in it too and thus we got an expanded story along with it. With Lilith, we're first told it's Adam, then we find out it's not Adam, then we find out humanity is referred to as "Lilim" and thus learn humanity spawned from Lilith. But wait! Then we also find out we spawned from the primordial soup called LCL! In many respects, Evangelion at that time was never really 'finished'. Conclusive? Yes, to a degree. At this point I find it more interesting that we're both so vehement about a badly animated cartoon whose plot is a word salad that came out of someone tossing a physics book and Hermeticism for Dummies into a blender. Not a regular blender, the kind that can grind up a stapler on YouTube. Anno et. al. never put anwhere near as much thought into how any of this shit works as we have.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Sep 11, 2014 1:23 am Oh, Chuckman is channeling my boundless hate for extraneous sources. No wonder I suddenly felt all itchy. But yeah, this getting stupid. Stop fighting girls, you're both pretty.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Sep 11, 2014 1:24 am Tell me how I'm pretty. Stroke my hair.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Thu Sep 11, 2014 3:15 am This combined with your avatar makes it triple the funny!

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Thu Sep 11, 2014 7:41 am Technically NGE 2 falls in either the 3rd or 4th tire of canonicity. Second tier canon is when the true god of Evangelion, Hideaki Anno, speaks.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Thu Sep 11, 2014 1:56 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: Technically NGE 2 falls in either the 3rd or 4th tire of canonicity. Second tier canon is when the true god of Evangelion, Hideaki Anno, speaks.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu Sep 11, 2014 2:24 pm I put NGE2 into a vague Tier 2.5 zone. What's this talk of "paragraphs" of information, anyway? It's a whole game (two versions of a game, even) consisting of TONS of text and images. The relevant stuff goes beyond the Classified Information that non-Japanese seem to default to thinking about when they hear "NGE2". In fact, the CI need the rest of the game to put some of their weirdness into context. I love the First Ancestral Race and everything the concept brings. I vaguely remember a time when speculation was done without the idea of alien progenitors lingering in the background, and the additional background has only enriched discussion, as far as I'm concerned. Without the hard sci-fi grounding, we'd probably still be neck-deep in speculation based on misinformed religious allegories. The FAR get a bad rap for "demystifying" NGE, telling us "too much", being "hokey" or "cliche". However, what is NGE, in the end? It isn't a story about gods; it's a story about people. The otherwise-inscrutable source of everything in the show is not some deity, but an ancient race of people who, like us, feared oblivion and didn't want to simply vanish when their time was up. Primordial human anxieties underlie the whole endeavor, and I find that poignant and beautiful.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Thu Sep 11, 2014 3:31 pm I agree with you, Reichu. As to your inquiry: I think people refer to the CI because that's what we can access. Despite your work on the alternate scenarios and such we can't access them directly, and they are, as the descriptor suggests, alternates. The CI seems to apply to the main NGE storyline, so that's why we focus on that.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Thu Sep 11, 2014 6:31 pm If the director of a film makes an objective statement about a film with regard to what it is he created, it is true. Ipso facto. Case-o closed-o. Argument null.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Sep 11, 2014 6:41 pm There's no deciding to be done. Everything has depth. You just have to look for it.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Thu Sep 11, 2014 6:42 pm If you have to really search for something in this context, you're searching for what it means to you and no longer for its objective meaning.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Sep 11, 2014 6:45 pm Right, there is no objective meaning.
